summary: I was born in India, which I consider my passport country and the root of my cultural identity. Even though we moved frequently, my parents made sure to keep Indian traditions alive by celebrating holidays, cooking Indian food, and keeping Indian texts at home. Returning briefly to India during our travels always felt like reconnecting with my heritage. India represents the foundation of who I am, despite living all over the world.

My family moved to Beijing, China, when I was a child, and we stayed there for almost five years. This was one of the earliest places I lived outside India, and it shaped my understanding of different cultures early on. Living in Beijing with my diplomat father exposed me to a new language and way of life, yet I never felt like a stranger because my family made every place feel like home. Beijing was an important chapter in my childhood migration story.

China was one of the first countries I lived in as a child, specifically in Beijing, where we spent nearly five years. Living there with my family gave me an early appreciation for cultural diversity and helped me understand that people are more than just their nationality. My time in China was part of a broader pattern of moving that shaped my global perspective. It taught me to look beyond labels and see the shared humanity in everyone.

After Beijing, my family moved to Saudi Arabia for a brief period during my childhood. Although our time there was short, it was another experience that contributed to my sense of being at home anywhere. Living in Saudi Arabia added to the mosaic of cultures I was exposed to, reinforcing my belief that home is defined by people, not places. This stay was part of the many moves my diplomat father’s career required.

We moved to Abu Dhabi in the United Arab Emirates after returning briefly to India, and it became another significant place in my childhood. Living there was part of the ongoing pattern of migration due to my father's diplomatic work. Abu Dhabi, like the other places, never felt foreign because my family’s love and cultural practices made it home. This experience further broadened my understanding of different cultures and people.

The United Arab Emirates became home for a time when we lived in Abu Dhabi during my childhood. This was one of several countries where my family’s diplomatic life took us, and it contributed to my global upbringing. Despite the many moves, my parents ensured that our Indian culture remained central to our identity. Living in the UAE helped me appreciate the diversity of cultures and the importance of people in defining home.

I came to the United States to pursue my Ph.D., and this is where I have stayed since. Moving here marked a new chapter in my life, transitioning from a childhood of moving around the world to settling down for my education and career. The U.S. has become a place where I use my experiences to educate others about culture and identity. It’s where I challenge students to think beyond labels and appreciate the complexity of people.
